name: Prateek Poonia
summary: Prateek Poonia from Sikar, Rajasthan, shares how setbacks in JEE and his first SSB conference became the foundation of an incredible comeback that saw him clear three consecutive SSBs and secure AIR 64 in NDA merit.
data:
# From JEE Failures to AIR 64 in NDA Merit – My Journey

I come from Sikar, Rajasthan, a place well known for producing engineers and doctors. Like most students around me, I prepared for JEE with the hope of getting into a prestigious engineering college. Unfortunately, things didn't go as planned. I couldn't score well in JEE, nor did I perform the way I had expected in several other competitive examinations. For a while, it felt as though I was falling behind everyone else.

But deep inside, I always knew that engineering was never my real dream.

My dream was to serve the nation as an officer in the Indian Navy.

Unlike many aspirants who face resistance from their families, I was fortunate. My parents stood firmly beside me from the very beginning. They believed in my dream even when my results in other examinations weren't encouraging. Their faith became my biggest strength whenever self-doubt started creeping in.

After missing out on opportunities elsewhere, I decided to give everything I had towards the NDA examination. In my first written attempt, I couldn't make it. It was disappointing, but instead of giving up, I went back, analyzed my mistakes, and prepared even harder.

The hard work paid off.

In my second NDA written attempt, I finally cleared the examination and received my SSB call.

I entered the SSB with confidence but also with the understanding that every candidate there was equally determined. The screening process went smoothly, and I found myself selected for the five-day assessment.

Throughout the psychological tests, GTO tasks, and interview, I simply focused on being myself. I wasn't trying to act like an officer—I wanted the assessors to see the person I truly was. I participated wholeheartedly in every group task, gave my best during the lecturette, enjoyed the outdoor obstacles, and learned something new from every activity.

However, when the results were announced, my chest number wasn't called.

I had reached the Conference Out stage.

For many aspirants, getting conferenced out is heartbreaking because you come so close to the finish line. That day hurt. I kept wondering what more I could have done.

But instead of treating it as a failure, I decided to treat it as feedback.

I worked on every weakness I had noticed—my communication, my confidence, my awareness, my personality, and even the smallest details that I had overlooked during my first SSB.

That conference out completely changed me.

I returned stronger, more mature, and far more confident than before.

The transformation was unbelievable.

I appeared for **NDA 155 SSB**, and this time, I was recommended.

Instead of stopping there, I continued appearing for my remaining entries.

Soon after, I cleared **TES-54 SSB**.

Then I went on to clear **Navy Technical Entry SSB** as well.

Three consecutive SSB recommendations.

Looking back, it almost feels unreal.

The same candidate who had once walked out after being conferenced out had now become someone capable of clearing three SSBs in a row.

Finally came the NDA merit list.

When I checked my result, I saw my name with **All India Rank 64**.

That moment is something I'll never forget.

All the disappointments from JEE, every setback, every rejection, every hour of preparation, every improvement I had made—they all suddenly felt worthwhile.

Today, I am proudly undergoing training at the National Defence Academy, living the dream that I had carried with me for years.

If there's one thing this journey has taught me, it's this:

Failures don't define your future.

Whether it's JEE, any other competitive examination, or even getting conferenced out in an SSB, none of these determine who you eventually become. What truly matters is your willingness to learn, improve, and return stronger.

Sometimes, success isn't about getting selected on your first opportunity.

Sometimes, it's about becoming the person capable of succeeding when the next opportunity arrives.

For me, that opportunity came after a conference out.

And it changed my life forever.
